heroOverview: Atelectasis (ICD-10: J98.11) is the collapse of part or all of a lung — a condition in which alveolar air sacs deflate and close, reducing the volume of ventilated lung tissue and impairing the transfer of oxygen into the bloodstream. Unlike pneumothorax, where air collects outside the lung, atelectasis is a failure of the lung itself to remain open, most commonly because a mucus plug, tumour, or external compression blocks or narrows the supplying airway and the air distal to the blockage is slowly absorbed. Post-operative atelectasis is the most common complication after general anaesthesia, affecting an estimated 90% of patients to some degree during surgery and a clinically significant minority in the days that follow — it is the leading cause of post-surgical fever in the first 48 hours and contributes to pneumonia, prolonged hospital stay, and respiratory failure. Beyond surgery, atelectasis occurs in asthma attacks, cystic fibrosis, lung cancer, pleural effusion, and any condition that restricts deep breathing. Management ranges from breathing exercises and physiotherapy for mild cases to bronchoscopy for airway clearance and, in some settings, positive pressure ventilation to re-expand the affected segments.

keyStats.prevalence: Post-operative atelectasis affects up to 90% of patients during general anaesthesia to some degree; clinically significant atelectasis requiring intervention occurs in approximately 5-10% of general surgical cases
keyStats.demographics: Affects all age groups; particularly common in post-operative patients, those on mechanical ventilation, smokers, and patients with cystic fibrosis or neuromuscular disease
keyStats.avgAge: Highly variable — post-operative atelectasis peaks in adults over 50 with lung disease; atelectasis from congenital or bronchogenic causes can occur in infants
keyStats.globalCases: The most common respiratory complication of surgery worldwide; exact case counts are not tracked separately but millions of cases occur annually given global surgical volumes

definition: Atelectasis (ICD-10 J98.11) is the complete or partial collapse of the alveoli — the microscopic air sacs where gas exchange occurs — resulting in reduced or absent ventilation of the affected lung region. The term derives from the Greek 'ateles' (incomplete) and 'ektasis' (expansion). Atelectasis is classified by mechanism rather than location: obstructive (resorption) atelectasis occurs when a mucus plug, tumour, aspirated foreign body, or endobronchial lesion blocks an airway and the trapped distal air is gradually absorbed by the bloodstream over hours; compressive (passive) atelectasis results from external compression of the lung by pleural effusion, pneumothorax, haemothorax, or a large thoracic mass; relaxation (loss of surfactant) atelectasis occurs when the pulmonary surfactant layer that normally reduces alveolar surface tension is depleted — the principal mechanism of respiratory distress syndrome in premature infants and a factor in ARDS; adhesive atelectasis (microatelectasis) is diffuse alveolar instability from surfactant dysfunction without a visible obstructing lesion; and cicatricial atelectasis is permanent collapse from fibrotic lung scarring after tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, or radiation. Physiologically, atelectatic lung receives blood flow but no ventilation, creating an intrapulmonary shunt that reduces arterial oxygen saturation — the degree of hypoxaemia is proportional to the fraction of total lung volume that is atelectatic. Large lobar or total lung atelectasis causes mediastinal shift toward the affected side (distinguishing it from pneumothorax, where the shift is away). Atelectasis is managed by chest physicians, respiratory therapists, and thoracic surgeons depending on the cause and severity.

typesClassification.description: The most common type in adults; a blocking lesion — mucus plug, tumour, aspirated foreign body, or airway oedema — prevents air entry and residual distal air is absorbed by the bloodstream, causing the lung segment to collapse. Classic post-operative scenario after general anaesthesia.

External compression of lung tissue by a large pleural effusion, pneumothorax, haemothorax, or abdominal distension pushes the lung into a collapsed state without any intrinsic airway obstruction. Resolves when the compressing pathology is treated.
Loss or dysfunction of surfactant reduces alveolar stability, causing diffuse small alveolar collapse. The primary mechanism in neonatal respiratory distress syndrome, ARDS, and post-bypass surgery.

Permanent lung collapse from parenchymal fibrosis after healed tuberculosis, radiation, or chronic granulomatous disease. Unlike other types, this is irreversible and does not respond to physiotherapy or bronchoscopy.

A specific form associated with asbestos-related pleural disease in which atelectatic lung folds on itself into a rounded mass adjacent to the pleura, mimicking a lung tumour on imaging. Also called Blesovsky syndrome.

primarySymptoms: Breathlessness or shortness of breath, ranging from mild dyspnoea on exertion in small atelectasis to severe resting dyspnoea when a whole lobe or lung collapses, as the functional respiratory surface is acutely reduced.

Rapid shallow breathing (tachypnoea) as the body compensates for reduced effective lung volume with a higher respiratory rate rather than larger tidal volumes that the collapsed lung cannot accommodate.
A dry or productive cough, particularly in obstructive atelectasis where retained secretions behind the blockage and irritation of the compressed airways drive the cough reflex.
Reduced or absent breath sounds over the affected lobe or segment on auscultation, because there is no airflow through the collapsed tissue to generate normal vesicular breath sounds.
Dullness to percussion over the atelectatic segment — the airless collapsed lung tissue transmits less resonance than normal air-filled lung.
Chest tightness or a sense of restricted breathing on the affected side, more prominent in lobar or total lung atelectasis than in small segmental collapse.

Fever within 24-48 hours of surgery — post-operative atelectasis is the most common cause of low-grade fever in the first two days after a general anaesthetic, attributed to local inflammatory cytokine release from collapsed, poorly-cleared alveoli.
Tachycardia (elevated heart rate), which accompanies hypoxaemia from ventilation-perfusion mismatch as the body attempts to maintain oxygen delivery to tissues despite reduced pulmonary oxygenation.
Visible chest asymmetry or reduced chest wall movement on the affected side in large atelectasis, with the affected side appearing to lag behind the normal side during inspiration.
Cyanosis of the lips or fingertips in severe cases where arterial oxygen saturation falls below approximately 85%, indicating critical hypoxaemia requiring urgent intervention.
earlyWarningSigns: A mild drop in oxygen saturation to 93-95% on pulse oximetry in a post-operative patient that was previously 98-99%, without an obvious cause.
Low-grade fever of 37.5-38.5°C in the first 24-48 hours after surgery without clear infectious source — post-operative atelectasis is the commonest explanation.
A new area of reduced breath sounds on ward examination that was not present at an earlier assessment.
A nagging unproductive cough that appears in the hours after extubation from general anaesthesia.
emergencySigns: Oxygen saturation below 88% on pulse oximetry despite supplemental oxygen via face mask, indicating severe hypoxaemia from large atelectasis or bilateral involvement.
Sudden complete collapse of an entire lung producing acute severe breathlessness, tracheal deviation toward the affected side, and haemodynamic compromise.
Rapidly worsening respiratory distress in a patient with a known mucus plug or endobronchial lesion — bronchoscopy is urgently needed to clear the airway before respiratory failure supervenes.
A child who inhales a foreign body and develops sudden choking followed by unilateral reduced breath sounds — this is an airway emergency requiring immediate bronchoscopy.

causes.cause: Post-operative mucus plugging and reduced deep breathing
causes.description: General anaesthesia impairs mucociliary transport, suppresses the cough reflex, and reduces surfactant production. Retained secretions accumulate in dependent airways, forming mucus plugs. Supine positioning, diaphragmatic splinting from abdominal incision pain, and reduced respiratory drive from opiate analgesia combine to prevent the deep breaths needed to clear plugs and re-expand collapsed alveoli. This mechanism accounts for the majority of clinically significant post-surgical atelectasis.

causes.cause: Endobronchial obstruction by mucus in airway disease
causes.description: In asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, bronchiectasis, and cystic fibrosis, excessive or abnormally thick mucus obstructs subsegmental and segmental bronchi. The resulting obstructive atelectasis often causes the lobular or segmental plate-like shadows on chest X-ray that are characteristic of these conditions during exacerbations.

Central lung tumours arising within or compressing a lobar or segmental bronchus produce progressive obstructive atelectasis distal to the blockage. Lobar or whole-lung collapse is a classic presenting finding of central squamous cell carcinoma; post-obstructive pneumonia in the collapsed lobe is a common consequence.

Particularly in children aged 1-4 years, inhaled food particles, small toys, or vegetable matter lodge in a bronchus (most commonly the right lower lobe bronchus due to the more vertical angle of the right main bronchus), producing obstructive atelectasis of the distal lung. Adults can aspirate liquid gastric contents or food under sedation or after neurological events.

Large pleural effusions — from heart failure, malignancy, infection, or hypoalbuminaemia — mechanically compress the adjacent lung parenchyma, producing passive compressive atelectasis of the lower lobes. The degree of atelectasis is proportional to effusion volume and resolves when the fluid is drained.

Intubated patients, particularly those in the intensive care unit, develop dependent atelectasis in the posterior lung segments because of the weight of the lung in the supine position, the relatively low tidal volumes used in lung-protective ventilation, and the absence of sighing (periodic deep breaths). Without regular recruitment manoeuvres, sustained dependent atelectasis can progress to ventilator-associated pneumonia.

riskFactors.factor: General anaesthesia and abdominal or thoracic surgery
riskFactors.description: The combination of anaesthetic agents suppressing respiratory drive, surgical positioning, post-operative pain limiting deep breathing, and opioid analgesia is the dominant risk factor for clinically significant atelectasis; upper abdominal and thoracic surgeries carry the highest risk (approximately 10-40% incidence of clinically significant post-operative atelectasis).

Smoking impairs mucociliary clearance, increases airway mucus production, and causes baseline airway inflammation; smokers have two to six times higher rates of post-operative pulmonary complications including atelectasis. Pre-operative smoking cessation for at least 4-8 weeks meaningfully reduces risk.

Abdominal obesity reduces functional residual capacity and diaphragmatic excursion, particularly in the supine position; obese patients have a substantially higher baseline closing volume, making alveolar collapse more likely during normal tidal breathing.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic lung disease (COPD, asthma, bronchiectasis, cystic fibrosis)
riskFactors.description: Pre-existing airway disease with increased mucus production, impaired clearance, or airway hyperreactivity predisposes to mucus plugging and recurrent atelectasis, both as a manifestation of the underlying disease and as a peri-operative complication.

Conditions that impair the cough reflex or respiratory muscle strength — including Guillain-Barré syndrome, motor neurone disease, spinal cord injury, stroke, or heavy sedation — allow secretions to accumulate and fail to generate the respiratory muscle effort needed for alveolar re-expansion.

Immobile patients in hospital breathe at lower tidal volumes, fail to generate the deep sighing breaths that normally recruit closed alveoli every few minutes, and accumulate secretions in dependent lung zones; early mobilisation after surgery is one of the most effective preventive interventions.

Tumours within or adjacent to a bronchus progressively narrow the airway lumen; atelectasis of the distal lung appears when blockage is sufficient to prevent air entry and residual air is absorbed. Lobar atelectasis in an older smoker should always prompt exclusion of endobronchial malignancy.

The immature lungs of preterm infants produce insufficient pulmonary surfactant before approximately 32-34 weeks of gestation, causing widespread alveolar instability and collapse — neonatal respiratory distress syndrome, the most severe form of atelectasis.

affectedDemographics: Post-operative patients, especially after abdominal, thoracic, and cardiac surgery — the highest-risk adult group
Patients on mechanical ventilation in intensive care units, in whom dependent atelectasis develops within hours of intubation
Older adults with COPD or smoking history undergoing surgery, who combine increased mucus production with post-operative respiratory depression
Children aged 1-4 years who inhale foreign bodies, the predominant cause of obstructive atelectasis in this age group
Premature neonates with surfactant deficiency (respiratory distress syndrome), the neonatal form of atelectasis
Patients with neuromuscular diseases — motor neurone disease, Duchenne muscular dystrophy, spinal cord injury — in whom progressive respiratory muscle weakness leads to recurrent atelectasis
Cancer patients with central endobronchial tumours causing lobar collapse, or with large malignant pleural effusions producing compressive atelectasis
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of atelectasis begins with clinical assessment and is confirmed by imaging. Post-operative patients are at sufficiently high risk that declining oxygen saturation, low-grade fever, or new reduced breath sounds over a lobe should prompt immediate clinical evaluation for atelectasis even without imaging. A plain chest X-ray is the standard first imaging study: it shows characteristic findings of atelectasis including increased density (whitening) in the affected area, volume loss with elevation of the ipsilateral hemidiaphragm, mediastinal shift toward the affected side (unlike pneumothorax, where shift is away), narrowing of the ipsilateral intercostal spaces, and displacement of the hilar shadow downward in lower lobe or upward in upper lobe atelectasis. In the post-operative setting, linear or plate-like streaks at the lung bases (discoid or Fleischner lines) are the most common and often the earliest finding, reflecting subsegmental collapse. CT of the chest is more sensitive than plain X-ray and precisely delineates the extent and mechanism of atelectasis — identifying endobronchial lesions, mucus plugs, compressive effusions, or fibrotic cicatrisation; CT is indicated when chest X-ray is inconclusive, when malignancy needs exclusion, or when atelectasis fails to resolve with standard treatment. In suspected endobronchial obstruction — particularly lobar collapse in a smoker with no obvious infectious or post-operative cause — bronchoscopy is both diagnostic and potentially therapeutic, allowing direct visualisation, biopsy of any lesion, and suction or lavage to remove a mucus plug. Pulse oximetry is essential for monitoring hypoxaemia severity. Arterial blood gases quantify the degree of ventilation-perfusion mismatch and guide oxygen therapy requirements. In children with suspected foreign body, bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia is the diagnostic and treatment procedure of choice; chest X-ray may show air trapping on the affected side (the obstructed lung does not deflate normally on expiration) before collapse supervenes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging for detecting atelectasis, showing increased density in the collapsed area, elevated hemidiaphragm, mediastinal shift, and the pattern of collapse (lobar, segmental, or discoid) that guides subsequent management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standing or portable supine chest X-ray takes 1-2 minutes. In post-operative patients, portable X-rays are performed at the bedside without moving the patient off the ward.

diagnosticTests.purpose: More sensitive than chest X-ray; identifies the exact cause (mucus plug, tumour, effusion, fibrosis), delineates which lobe or segment is affected, and distinguishes atelectasis from consolidation or malignancy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-10 minute scan in the CT scanner; contrast injection through a vein may be added to identify tumours or vascular complications. Results available within an hour.

diagnosticTests.test: Pulse oximetry and oxygen saturation monitoring
diagnosticTests.purpose: Continuous non-invasive monitoring of arterial oxygen saturation to detect hypoxaemia early in at-risk patients, and to assess response to treatment interventions such as physiotherapy and supplemental oxygen.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clip-on sensor placed on a finger or earlobe measures oxygen saturation continuously without any needle or procedure. Standard monitoring in all post-operative and ICU patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures PaO2, PaCO2, and pH to quantify the severity of hypoxaemia and any hypercapnia (CO2 retention) in severe atelectasis, guiding the intensity of respiratory support required.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small blood sample taken from the radial artery at the wrist under local anaesthetic; results available within 15 minutes from an on-site analyser.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualisation of the airway to identify and remove mucus plugs, obtain biopsies of endobronchial lesions, and perform therapeutic lavage in refractory atelectasis; simultaneously diagnostic and therapeutic when the cause is endobronchial obstruction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin flexible camera is passed through the nostril or mouth under local anaesthetic and sedation. The procedure takes 15-30 minutes. You may have a mild sore throat or hoarseness afterwards for a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the degree of functional restriction in chronic recurrent atelectasis, differentiates obstructive from restrictive patterns, and provides a baseline for monitoring treatment response over time.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A series of maximal breathing effort manoeuvres into a spirometer; the technician coaches you through each one. Full lung volume testing takes approximately 30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects air trapping in suspected foreign body aspiration in children: the atelectatic lobe appears fixed in volume on expiration while the normal lung deflates, or the mediastinum shifts away from the blocked side on expiration.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two chest X-rays are taken — one during deep inspiration and one during full expiration — so that differences in lung volume between the two sides can be compared. Fluoroscopy provides a real-time moving image instead.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of atelectasis is directed at the underlying cause and at re-expanding the collapsed lung. In post-operative and bed-bound patients where mucus plugging is the mechanism, first-line treatment is respiratory physiotherapy: deep breathing exercises, incentive spirometry (breathing against a resistance to maximise inspiratory volume), directed coughing, the active cycle of breathing technique, and postural drainage. Early mobilisation — getting patients out of bed and walking as soon as safely possible after surgery — is one of the most effective preventive and therapeutic measures. Supplemental oxygen corrects hypoxaemia but does not treat the underlying collapse; it should be titrated to maintain SpO2 ≥ 94% while other measures address the mechanical cause. When mucus plugging fails to respond to physiotherapy in an intubated patient, endotracheal suctioning, recruitment manoeuvres (brief increases in airway pressure to re-inflate collapsed alveoli), and positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) are used by the ICU team. Nebulised hypertonic saline, mucolytics such as N-acetylcysteine, and inhaled bronchodilators reduce mucus viscosity and relieve bronchospasm to facilitate secretion clearance. In non-intubated patients with lobar or whole-lung collapse from mucus plugging unresponsive to physiotherapy, flexible bronchoscopy with therapeutic lavage or suction is highly effective, achieving re-expansion in approximately 80-90% of cases. Compressive atelectasis from pleural effusion or pneumothorax resolves once the compressing fluid or air is drained — the atelectatic lung then re-expands passively. Endobronchial tumour causing lobar collapse requires oncological management — surgical resection, radiotherapy, endobronchial stenting, or laser debulking — to relieve the obstruction. Foreign body aspiration requires rigid bronchoscopy for removal in most cases, particularly in children. Cicatricial atelectasis from fibrosis is permanent and does not respond to any of these measures; management focuses on optimising remaining lung function. For high-risk surgical patients, pre-operative optimisation including smoking cessation, treatment of airway disease, and incentive spirometry training reduces post-operative atelectasis incidence by approximately 30-50%.

medicalTreatments.name: Physiotherapy: incentive spirometry and deep breathing exercises
medicalTreatments.description: The patient inhales slowly and deeply against a calibrated resistance device (incentive spirometer) to maximise inspiratory volume and recruit collapsed alveoli. Combined with directed coughing, active cycle of breathing, and postural drainage, physiotherapy is the cornerstone of treatment for post-operative atelectasis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Incentive spirometry reduces post-operative pulmonary complications by approximately 50% compared with no intervention in high-risk patients after upper abdominal surgery when performed 10 breaths per hour while awake.

medicalTreatments.name: Supplemental oxygen therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Administered via nasal cannula, face mask, or high-flow nasal oxygen (Optiflow) to correct hypoxaemia while underlying atelectasis is treated. High-flow nasal oxygen provides a mild positive end-expiratory pressure effect that modestly assists alveolar re-expansion in spontaneously breathing patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: High-flow nasal oxygen reduces post-extubation hypoxaemia by approximately 25% compared with standard face mask oxygen, and reduces re-intubation rates in high-risk ICU patients, providing both oxygenation support and a mild recruitment benefit.

medicalTreatments.name: Bronchodilators (SABA and LABA)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-acting beta-2 agonists (salbutamol) and anticholinergic agents (ipratropium) relieve bronchospasm contributing to obstructive atelectasis in patients with asthma or COPD, and improve mucociliary clearance. Nebulised delivery is preferred in acutely breathless patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Bronchodilators reduce bronchospasm-related atelectasis in asthma and COPD by improving airway calibre; their benefit in atelectasis without underlying bronchospasm is limited to facilitation of secretion clearance.

medicalTreatments.name: Mucolytic agents (N-acetylcysteine, dornase alfa in cystic fibrosis)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce mucus viscosity to facilitate clearance of obstructing plugs. Inhaled dornase alfa specifically cleaves the DNA released by neutrophils in cystic fibrosis sputum, producing dramatic viscosity reduction. N-acetylcysteine is used in non-CF mucosal plugging.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Dornase alfa reduces pulmonary exacerbations by approximately 22-34% in cystic fibrosis patients with established lung disease, and significantly reduces the frequency of atelectatic episodes in this population.

medicalTreatments.name: Positive pressure ventilation (CPAP, BiPAP, mechanical ventilation)
medicalTreatments.description: Non-invasive positive pressure ventilation via CPAP or BiPAP mask provides a stenting effect on collapsed alveoli, re-expanding microatelectasis without intubation. Mechanical ventilation with PEEP is used in intubated ICU patients; recruitment manoeuvres (sustained inflation to 30-40 cmH2O for 30-40 seconds) provide immediate alveolar re-expansion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prophylactic CPAP after cardiac surgery reduces clinically significant atelectasis by approximately 40-50% compared with standard care. Lung recruitment manoeuvres improve PaO2/FiO2 ratio by 30-60 mmHg acutely in ARDS, though sustained benefit requires adequate PEEP.

medicalTreatments.name: Flexible bronchoscopy with therapeutic lavage
medicalTreatments.description: Direct endobronchial suction and lavage to remove obstructing mucus plugs, blood clots, or inspissated secretions from segmental and lobar bronchi in patients with refractory atelectasis unresponsive to physiotherapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Bronchoscopic suction and lavage achieves radiological improvement or resolution in approximately 80-90% of cases of lobar atelectasis from mucus plugging in intensive care and post-operative settings.

surgicalOptions.name: Rigid bronchoscopy for foreign body removal
surgicalOptions.description: Gold standard for extracting foreign bodies from the airway, particularly in children. A rigid bronchoscope provides a wide working channel for grasping and removing solid objects and for controlling airway bleeding. Performed under general anaesthesia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful removal of aspirated foreign bodies in approximately 95-98% of cases with rigid bronchoscopy in experienced paediatric bronchoscopists; failure rates below 5% in large series.

surgicalOptions.name: Endobronchial stenting or laser debulking for tumour
surgicalOptions.description: In inoperable central airway tumours causing lobar atelectasis, an endobronchial metallic or silicone stent opens the obstructed bronchus, or laser/electrocautery debulking removes intraluminal tumour. Palliative interventions that restore ventilation to the collapsed lobe.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Endobronchial stenting achieves immediate airway patency in approximately 70-90% of cases; re-expansion of the previously collapsed lobe occurs in a subset, depending on duration of collapse and parenchymal viability.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ambulation and positioning
alternativeTreatments.description: Early mobilisation, sitting upright, and positioning patients on their side or prone increases functional residual capacity, improves ventilation of dependent lung zones, and mobilises secretions more effectively than bed rest; the simplest and safest preventive and therapeutic intervention.

alternativeTreatments.name: Intermittent positive pressure breathing (IPPB)
alternativeTreatments.description: A form of assisted positive-pressure breathing administered via a mouthpiece for 10-20 minutes; delivers larger tidal volumes than spontaneous effort in patients who cannot perform effective incentive spirometry unaided, used in neuromuscular disease and post-surgical fatigue.

alternativeTreatments.name: Flutter valve and oscillatory PEP devices
alternativeTreatments.description: Hand-held oscillatory positive expiratory pressure devices (Flutter, Acapella) generate oscillatory airflow during exhalation to loosen secretions and facilitate their clearance; useful adjuncts for chronic atelectasis in COPD, bronchiectasis, and cystic fibrosis.

whySeeSpecialist: A respiratory physician (pulmonologist) should be consulted when atelectasis does not resolve with standard physiotherapy within 48-72 hours, when the cause is unclear, when lobar or whole-lung collapse develops in a non-post-operative setting, or when bronchoscopy is being considered. In children, any suspected foreign body aspiration requires immediate referral to a paediatric bronchoscopist. When atelectasis is caused or suspected to be caused by an endobronchial tumour, urgent referral to a thoracic oncologist for staging and treatment planning is essential.
doctorSelectionGuide: For post-operative atelectasis, a respiratory physiotherapist supervised by the ward chest physician or intensivist manages most cases. For complex or recurrent atelectasis, a pulmonologist with bronchoscopy expertise is the appropriate specialist. For foreign body aspiration in children, a paediatric pulmonologist or paediatric ENT surgeon skilled in rigid bronchoscopy is required. For atelectasis from endobronchial malignancy, an interventional pulmonologist and thoracic oncologist working together provide the best technical and oncological management.
preventionStrategies: Pre-operative respiratory physiotherapy training — learning incentive spirometry and active cycle of breathing before surgery and practising them immediately after — reduces post-operative atelectasis by approximately 30-50%.
Smoking cessation at least 4-8 weeks before elective surgery reduces mucus production and improves ciliary function, significantly lowering peri-operative pulmonary complication rates.
Early mobilisation after surgery: getting out of bed on the day of operation (where safe) is the single most effective post-operative measure.

Adequate post-operative analgesia — particularly epidural or regional nerve blocks for thoracic and abdominal surgery — allows deep breathing and effective coughing that would otherwise be suppressed by incision pain.
Lung-protective ventilation strategies (tidal volumes of 6-8 mL/kg ideal body weight and appropriate PEEP) in mechanically ventilated patients minimise ventilator-induced atelectasis.
Routine incentive spirometry — 10 maximal inspiratory efforts per hour while awake — in all high-risk post-operative patients.
lifestyleModifications: Stop smoking: this is the most impactful modifiable risk factor for post-operative and recurrent atelectasis.
Maintain a healthy weight: obesity significantly increases the risk of post-operative atelectasis by reducing functional residual capacity and diaphragmatic excursion.
Perform regular aerobic exercise to maintain respiratory muscle strength and cardiopulmonary reserve.
Avoid prolonged bed rest: move and change position regularly during any period of reduced activity.
Manage chronic airway conditions (asthma, COPD, bronchiectasis) optimally with prescribed medications to minimise mucus production and bronchospasm.
Ensure annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccination to prevent respiratory infections that can precipitate mucus plugging and atelectasis.

recommended: Adequate hydration to keep airway secretions mobile and easier to clear
High-protein diet during post-operative recovery to support respiratory muscle function and wound healing
Antioxidant-rich foods including fruit and vegetables to support immune function during recovery
Small frequent meals in patients with severe restriction where large meals displace the diaphragm
avoid: Smoking and all tobacco products — paramount for respiratory health
Sedating substances including alcohol and benzodiazepines without medical indication, which suppress the cough reflex and respiratory drive
Foods that increase aspiration risk (poorly chewed solids, thin liquids) in patients with swallowing disorders who are prone to aspiration-related atelectasis

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise is encouraged in all patients with chronic or recurrent atelectasis to maintain respiratory muscle strength, improve diaphragmatic function, and reduce mucus retention. Walking, cycling, and swimming are all suitable. In the immediate post-operative period, deep breathing exercises and short walks on the ward from the first post-operative day are the priority; full exercise capacity is gradually restored over four to six weeks. Patients with cystic fibrosis, bronchiectasis, or neuromuscular disease benefit from supervised pulmonary rehabilitation, which integrates airway clearance techniques with graded exercise. During an acute atelectatic episode, vigorous exercise should wait until the collapse has resolved and oxygen saturation is maintained above 94%.
dailyManagement: Practise incentive spirometry or active cycle of breathing 10 repetitions every hour while awake in the post-operative period or during any acute atelectatic episode.
Change position frequently — alternate sitting upright, lying on each side, and walking — to ventilate different lung zones and prevent secretion pooling.
Take prescribed bronchodilators and mucolytic agents at the prescribed times; do not stop them during episodes of worsening breathlessness.
Report any new fever, worsening breathlessness, purulent sputum, or declining oxygen saturation to the medical team promptly.
For patients with cystic fibrosis or bronchiectasis, maintain the daily airway clearance regimen (physiotherapy, positive expiratory pressure device, nebulised DNase) even on days without symptoms.
prognosis: The prognosis of atelectasis depends almost entirely on the underlying cause and on the speed and completeness of treatment. Post-operative atelectasis in otherwise healthy patients resolves completely in the vast majority of cases within 24-72 hours of effective physiotherapy and mobilisation, with no long-term consequences. When mucus plug atelectasis in the post-operative period is not recognised and treated, secondary infection producing pneumonia complicates approximately 10-40% of cases, substantially increasing hospital stay and mortality risk in vulnerable patients. Lobar atelectasis caused by an endobronchial tumour has the prognosis of the underlying malignancy — re-expansion of the collapsed lobe by stenting improves dyspnoea and reduces infection risk but does not alter the overall cancer prognosis. In cystic fibrosis and bronchiectasis, atelectatic episodes are markers of disease activity and are associated with accelerated lung function decline; each exacerbation with atelectasis treated promptly and fully maintains better long-term function than undertreated exacerbations. Cicatricial (fibrotic) atelectasis is permanent and does not recover; management focuses on maximising function in remaining normal lung. Foreign body atelectasis in children treated promptly by bronchoscopy has an excellent prognosis; delayed diagnosis allows secondary infection and in rare cases permanent structural damage to the affected lobe.
recoveryTimeline: Post-operative discoid or segmental atelectasis typically begins improving within hours of starting physiotherapy and resolves radiologically within one to three days. Lobar atelectasis from mucus plugging, once treated by bronchoscopy, re-expands over one to seven days depending on the duration of collapse. Compressive atelectasis from pleural effusion resolves within 24-48 hours of effective drainage. Atelectasis from foreign body aspiration resolves within days of bronchoscopic removal, though secondary infection may require two to four weeks of antibiotics to clear. Chronic recurrent atelectasis in cystic fibrosis or bronchiectasis does not permanently resolve but is controlled with regular physiotherapy; each acute episode typically takes five to fourteen days to resolve with intensive treatment.
complications: Post-obstructive pneumonia — bacteria colonising the poorly-ventilated atelectatic segment multiply rapidly; pneumonia complicates approximately 10-40% of untreated lobar atelectasis and requires antibiotics in addition to airway clearance.

Lung abscess — suppurative infection of the collapsed segment can progress to abscess formation, requiring prolonged antibiotics and sometimes drainage, particularly in aspirated foreign body cases or in immunocompromised patients.

Respiratory failure — large bilateral atelectasis or lobar atelectasis superimposed on pre-existing lung disease can produce hypoxaemia severe enough to require supplemental oxygen, non-invasive ventilation, or intubation.

Permanent lung damage — prolonged lobar atelectasis, particularly from central tumour, can lead to bronchiectasis, pulmonary fibrosis, or cicatricial collapse in the affected lobe that does not reverse even after the obstruction is relieved.
Pleural empyema — infected secretions in the atelectatic segment can rupture into the pleural space, producing empyema requiring chest drain drainage and antibiotics; a rare but serious complication.

Post-obstructive pneumonitis — sterile inflammation in the atelectatic lung distal to a slow-growing tumour produces a radiographic density that can be mistaken for infection; distinguishing this from pneumonia guides appropriate management.

supportResources.name: NHLBI — How the Lungs Work
supportResources.description: Patient-facing resource from the U.S. National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute explaining lung anatomy, function, and common respiratory conditions including atelectasis.

supportResources.name: American Thoracic Society — Patient Education Series
supportResources.description: ATS patient resources covering lung conditions, respiratory therapies, and pulmonary rehabilitation — relevant for patients managing chronic atelectasis.

supportResources.name: NHS — Breathing exercises for lung conditions
supportResources.description: NHS guidance on deep breathing and physiotherapy techniques used to prevent and treat atelectasis, pneumonia, and post-operative lung complications.

sources.title: Duggan M and Kavanagh BP. Pulmonary atelectasis: a pathogenic perioperative entity. Anesthesiology 2005
Tusman G et al. Pulmonary atelectasis in anaesthesia and intensive care. Curr Opin Anaesthesiol 2012

confusedWithConditions.name: Pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pneumonia is active alveolar infection causing fever, productive cough, and airspace consolidation with air bronchograms on CT; atelectasis is alveolar collapse causing volume loss and mediastinal shift toward the affected side, without infectious exudate. The two co-occur frequently and can be hard to distinguish without CT.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pleural effusion
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A pleural effusion is fluid in the space outside the lung, producing a homogeneous opacity with a meniscus sign and no volume loss; atelectasis produces density within or adjacent to the lung with volume loss and diaphragm elevation. CT distinguishes them clearly.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ARDS produces diffuse bilateral alveolar infiltrates from a systemic inflammatory trigger and is not reversible with simple physiotherapy; atelectasis is focal or lobar collapse with a mechanical cause that responds to airway clearance or drainage of a compressing collection.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lung cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Central lung cancers cause lobar atelectasis as a complication; atelectasis is the finding that prompts investigation for the cancer. Rounded atelectasis (Blesovsky syndrome) can mimic a pulmonary nodule on chest X-ray and requires CT to distinguish benign pleural folding from a tumour.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Atelectasis and pneumonia are closely related: atelectasis impairs secretion clearance, predisposing to bacterial infection and pneumonia, while pneumonia consolidation can cause compressive atelectasis in adjacent segments.

relatedConditions.name: Lung cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Central endobronchial lung tumours are a significant non-infectious cause of lobar atelectasis in adults; lobar collapse on chest X-ray in a smoker requires urgent CT to exclude malignancy.

relatedConditions.name: Bronchiectasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Bronchiectasis produces thick retained secretions that cause recurrent lobar and segmental atelectasis; atelectasis episodes are markers of disease exacerbation and accelerate the structural lung damage of bronchiectasis.

relatedConditions.name: Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)
relatedConditions.relevance: Diffuse alveolar collapse from surfactant dysfunction is central to ARDS pathophysiology; recruitment manoeuvres and PEEP used to treat ARDS-related atelectasis are the most aggressive form of atelectasis management in the ICU.

faqs.question: What is atelectasis?
faqs.answer: Atelectasis is the collapse of part or all of a lung — the alveolar air sacs deflate and close, reducing the functional breathing surface. It is the most common lung complication after surgery and can also occur from mucus plugging, airway tumours, pleural effusion, or surfactant loss.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of atelectasis?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are shortness of breath, rapid shallow breathing, and reduced breath sounds over the collapsed lobe. Post-operative atelectasis often causes low-grade fever in the first 48 hours. Severe lobar or whole-lung collapse causes significant breathing difficulty and low oxygen levels.

faqs.question: What causes atelectasis after surgery?
faqs.answer: General anaesthesia impairs the cough reflex, reduces surfactant production, and suppresses deep breathing. Post-operative pain limits respiratory effort, and patients in the supine position accumulate secretions in lower airways. The combination of these factors causes mucus plugging and alveolar collapse in the hours after surgery.

faqs.question: How is atelectasis treated?
faqs.answer: Most post-operative atelectasis is treated with breathing exercises — incentive spirometry, deep breathing, and directed coughing — combined with early mobilisation. Supplemental oxygen corrects low blood oxygen. Severe mucus plugging unresponsive to physiotherapy is treated by bronchoscopy to clear the obstruction.

faqs.question: Can atelectasis cause a fever?
faqs.answer: Yes. Post-operative atelectasis is the most common cause of low-grade fever (under 38.5°C) in the first 24-48 hours after surgery, caused by local inflammatory mediators released from collapsed lung tissue. Fever above 38.5°C after 48 hours is more likely from infection, which atelectasis predisposes to.

faqs.question: Is atelectasis dangerous?
faqs.answer: Small atelectasis in an otherwise healthy patient is rarely dangerous and resolves with physiotherapy. Large lobar or bilateral atelectasis causes significant hypoxaemia and, if untreated, progresses to pneumonia or respiratory failure. In premature infants, surfactant-deficiency atelectasis is life-threatening without surfactant replacement therapy.

faqs.question: How can I prevent atelectasis after surgery?
faqs.answer: Learning and practising incentive spirometry before surgery, stopping smoking at least 4-8 weeks beforehand, getting out of bed and walking on the same day as surgery where safe, and ensuring adequate pain control to allow deep breathing are the most effective preventive steps.

faqs.question: Does atelectasis go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Small discoid atelectasis often resolves with normal breathing and movement without specific treatment. Larger lobar collapse, especially from a persistent mucus plug or tumour, does not resolve spontaneously and requires active airway clearance, bronchoscopy, or treatment of the underlying cause.

faqs.question: What is the difference between atelectasis and pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Atelectasis is collapsed airless lung without infection; pneumonia is active lung infection with inflammatory exudate filling the alveoli. Atelectasis produces volume loss with mediastinal shift; pneumonia produces consolidation without significant volume change. They can coexist, since collapsed atelectatic lung is prone to infection.

faqs.question: Can a child inhale something and develop atelectasis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Foreign body aspiration is a common cause of atelectasis in children aged 1-4. A swallowed toy part, piece of food, or vegetable matter lodges in a bronchus and blocks air entry. The child may have a sudden choking episode followed by persistent unilateral wheeze or reduced breath sounds. Rigid bronchoscopy under anaesthesia removes the object.

faqs.question: What is lobar atelectasis?
faqs.answer: Lobar atelectasis is the complete collapse of an entire lobe of a lung, typically from complete blockage of the lobar bronchus by a mucus plug or tumour. It produces a large opacification on chest X-ray with elevation of the diaphragm and mediastinal shift toward the affected side.

faqs.question: Can lung cancer cause atelectasis?
faqs.answer: Yes. A central lung cancer growing within or pressing on a lobar bronchus causes progressive obstructive atelectasis of the lobe it supplies. Lobar collapse on a chest X-ray in an older smoker is an important warning sign that warrants urgent CT and, if confirmed, bronchoscopy and biopsy.

faqs.question: What does atelectasis look like on a chest X-ray?
faqs.answer: Atelectasis appears as increased whiteness (opacity) in the affected lobe or segment, with characteristic volume loss — elevation of the diaphragm, mediastinal shift toward the affected side, and narrowing of the intercostal spaces on that side. Discoid atelectasis appears as thin horizontal white lines at the lung bases.

faqs.question: What is rounded atelectasis?
faqs.answer: Rounded atelectasis is a type of atelectasis associated with asbestos-related pleural disease, where the atelectatic lung folds into a rounded mass adjacent to the pleura. On chest X-ray it can look alarming but is benign; CT with a 'comet tail' of vessels sweeping into the mass is the diagnostic sign distinguishing it from a tumour.

faqs.question: How long does it take for atelectasis to resolve?
faqs.answer: Small post-operative atelectasis often improves within 24-72 hours of active physiotherapy and mobilisation. Lobar atelectasis from mucus plugging treated by bronchoscopy re-expands over one to seven days. Atelectasis from pleural effusion resolves within 24-48 hours of drainage. Permanent cicatricial atelectasis from lung fibrosis does not resolve.

faqs.question: Can atelectasis cause permanent lung damage?
faqs.answer: Persistent lobar atelectasis — especially from long-standing tumour obstruction — can lead to irreversible parenchymal changes including bronchiectasis, fibrosis, or permanent collapse even after the obstruction is relieved. Prompt treatment minimises the risk of permanent damage.

faqs.question: Is atelectasis the same as a collapsed lung?
faqs.answer: Atelectasis specifically refers to collapse of the lung tissue itself (the alveolar air sacs). A collapsed lung is an informal term that can describe atelectasis, but is also used for pneumothorax (air outside the lung compressing it from outside). They are different conditions with different causes and treatments.

faqs.question: What is incentive spirometry and does it help atelectasis?
faqs.answer: An incentive spirometer is a simple device that encourages deep slow maximal inhalations by providing visual feedback on inspiratory volume. Used 10 times per hour while awake in post-operative patients, it helps recruit collapsed alveoli by generating the deep breaths that normal breathing suppresses after surgery. Evidence supports a 50% reduction in post-operative pulmonary complications.

faqs.question: Can atelectasis affect both lungs at once?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bilateral atelectasis occurs in ARDS, after bilateral thoracic surgery, in severe asthma attacks, and in prolonged mechanical ventilation without adequate recruitment. Bilateral involvement causes more severe hypoxaemia than unilateral atelectasis and requires more aggressive management including non-invasive or mechanical ventilation.

faqs.question: Do I need a bronchoscopy for atelectasis?
faqs.answer: Not always. Most post-operative and mucus-plug atelectasis resolves with physiotherapy and mobilisation alone. Bronchoscopy is reserved for lobar atelectasis that does not respond within 24-48 hours of active physiotherapy, for suspected endobronchial tumour, and for children with foreign body aspiration.

metaDescription: Atelectasis is lung collapse causing breathlessness and low oxygen, most often after surgery. Learn causes, breathing exercises, bronchoscopy and recovery.
